target.title: Allow Skill Workshop apply through trusted skill symlinks
target.body:
## Summary
- Keep trusted symlink target configuration in one place with `skills.load.allowSymlinkTargets`.
- Add `skills.workshop.allowSymlinkTargetWrites` as the separate default-false switch for Skill Workshop writes through those already trusted targets.
- Thread active config into CLI, gateway, and agent-tool apply paths.
- Validate both `SKILL.md` and proposal support-file writes/removes through the same symlink write-target resolver.
- Add regression coverage and docs for trusted, read-only trusted, untrusted, and support-file symlink behavior.

## Tests
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/skills/workshop/service.test.ts src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts`
- `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/skills/workshop/store.ts src/skills/workshop/service.ts src/skills/workshop/service.test.ts`
- `node_modules/.bin/oxlint src/skills/workshop/store.ts src/skills/workshop/service.ts src/skills/workshop/service.test.ts`
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`
- `$HOME/.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main` -> clean, no accepted/actionable findings
- Testbox-through-Crabbox current-head proof: provider `blacksmith-testbox`, id `tbx_01ktvesre39fhhqj4c3z42et6a`, Actions run `27351180098`, head `8cd27f37b12175e9103bf9bf73996244caabe6b1`

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Skill Workshop can load/propose against skills under trusted symlink targets from `skills.load.allowSymlinkTargets`, but apply-time writes through those targets remain blocked unless `skills.workshop.allowSymlinkTargetWrites` is explicitly `true`. Untrusted symlink targets and tampered support-file targets remain blocked before writing.
- Real environment tested: Linux Blacksmith Testbox through Crabbox, Node `24.13.0`, current PR head `8cd27f37b12175e9103bf9bf73996244caabe6b1`, isolated temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` and `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH` per scenario. Each scenario used real filesystem directory symlinks and the real `node scripts/run-node.mjs skills workshop ...` CLI entrypoint.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Ran focused regression tests remotely: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/skills/workshop/service.test.ts src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts`.
  2. Created an isolated workspace where `workspace/skills` pointed to an external target and no `skills.load.allowSymlinkTargets` was configured; proposed a create with `PROPOSAL.md` plus `references/details.md`; verified apply failed with `untrusted symlink target` and wrote neither file.
  3. Created an isolated workspace where `workspace/skills` pointed to a target listed in `skills.load.allowSymlinkTargets`, with no Workshop write switch; verified `propose-update shared-skill` succeeded, then apply failed with `allowSymlinkTargetWrites` and preserved old `SKILL.md` plus support content.
  4. Created the same trusted symlink setup with `skills.workshop.allowSymlinkTargetWrites: true`; verified apply succeeded and wrote both `SKILL.md` and `references/details.md` into the real trusted target.
  5. Created a trusted `workspace/skills` symlink and an untrusted sibling `workspace/other-skills` symlink; proposed a create with support files, tampered the persisted proposal record so `target.skillDir` pointed through the untrusted sibling while `target.skillFile` stayed under the trusted target, and verified apply failed with `untrusted symlink target` without writing support files or `SKILL.md`.
- Evidence after fix (terminal capture):
```text
CRABBOX_PHASE:tests
REMOTE_HEAD 8cd27f37b12175e9103bf9bf73996244caabe6b1
[test] passed 2 Vitest shards in 7.82s
src/skills/workshop/service.test.ts: 25 passed
src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts: 23 passed

CRABBOX_PHASE:cli-proof
PROOF no_allowlist_blocked id=no-allowlist-skill-20260611-43d5b0340b
PROOF allowlist_loads_but_write_blocked id=shared-skill-20260611-429d46fa49
PROOF allowlist_write_enabled_applied id=shared-skill-20260611-c76a973654
PROOF tampered_support_target_blocked id=support-escape-20260611-b85d70d5b6
PROOF_COMPLETE current-head symlink workshop CLI proof passed

blacksmith run summary sync=delegated command=2m14.007s total=2m19.707s exit=0
{"provider":"blacksmith-testbox","leaseId":"tbx_01ktvesre39fhhqj4c3z42et6a","syncDelegated":true,"exitCode":0}
```
- Observed result after fix: The read trust list alone permits loading/proposal creation but not writes; enabling the Workshop switch permits writes only through already trusted targets; untrusted symlink targets and tampered support-file targets fail before any target file is created or modified.
- What was not tested: Live gateway delivery, Discord delivery, and a live model-driven agent-tool turn were not exercised in this proof run; the shared CLI/service write boundary and config schema/help paths were exercised directly, and PR CI is covering the broader repository gates for the pushed head.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The proof used isolated temporary OpenClaw state/config so it did not mutate operator or contributor workspaces.

target.title: fix(telegram): silently skip empty-text sends
target.body:
Supersedes #59725. The original PR was auto-closed on 2026-04-02 by openclaw-barnacle for the 10-open-PR cap. Reopening it programmatically fails because the close `stateReason` is `COMPLETED` plus the branch was force-pushed since, so this is a fresh PR carrying the rebased fix. ## Behavior addressed Interrupted mid-reply turns can produce formatted Telegram HTML that has no visible text and no plain-text fallback, for example an empty supported tag such as ``. Telegram rejects that payload with HTTP 400 (`message text is empty` legacy wording, `text must be non-empty` newer wording). Previously this surfaced as a user-visible delivery failure; this PR silently treats that empty-text send as a no-op. Parse errors remain out of scope and still throw. Live Telegram proof below shows bare `
` payloads currently fail as parse errors, while ` ` payloads are accepted by Telegram and still deliver normally. ## Change - `sendTelegramText` now returns `Promise` so it can report a skipped no-op send without inventing a Telegram message id. - When formatted HTML trims empty and the plain fallback also trims empty, `sendTelegramText` skips before calling Telegram. - When Telegram returns an empty-text 400 and there is no plain fallback, `sendTelegramText` logs verbosely and returns `undefined`. - Unrelated Telegram failures and parse errors still throw unchanged. - Reply accounting now ignores `undefined` send results, so skipped chunks do not advance reply state, delivered count, transcript mirroring, `message_sent.success`, or pinning. - Voice fallback chunking keeps reply/quote/button state available for the next real delivered chunk after a skipped empty-text chunk. ## Live Telegram round-trip proof Driver script `tsx`-imported the source-tree `sendTelegramText` from this PR branch and drove it through a real grammY `Bot` against the live Telegram Bot API. Token and chat id were redacted. Raw Telegram baseline: ``` payload="
" expected=parse-error -> error_code=400 description="Bad Request: can't parse entities: Unsupported start tag \"br\" at byte offset 0" payload="
" expected=parse-error -> error_code=400 description="Bad Request: can't parse entities: Unsupported start tag \"br/\" at byte offset 0" payload="
" expected=parse-error -> error_code=400 description="Bad Request: can't parse entities: Unsupported start tag \"br\" at byte offset 0" payload=" " expected=accepted-by-telegram -> OK message_id=10657 payload="   " expected=accepted-by-telegram -> OK message_id=10658 payload="" expected=empty-text -> error_code=400 description="Bad Request: text must be non-empty" ``` Source-tree `sendTelegramText(bot, chatId, payload, runtime, { textMode: "html", plainText: "" })`: ``` payload="
" expected=parse-error -> THREW error_code=400 description="Bad Request: can't parse entities: Unsupported start tag \"br\" at byte offset 0" payload="
" expected=parse-error -> THREW error_code=400 description="Bad Request: can't parse entities: Unsupported start tag \"br/\" at byte offset 0" payload="
" expected=parse-error -> THREW error_code=400 description="Bad Request: can't parse entities: Unsupported start tag \"br\" at byte offset 0" payload=" " expected=accepted-by-telegram -> resolved=10659 payload="   " expected=accepted-by-telegram -> resolved=10660 payload="" expected=empty-text -> resolved=undefined ``` A well-formed payload still delivered with real Telegram `message_id=10661`. ## Tests ``` node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/telegram/src/bot node scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs --only=extensions --threads=4 env -u OPENAI_API_KEY -u ANTHROPIC_API_KEY pnpm ui:i18n:check node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.ui.config.ts --reporter=verbose pnpm build PATH="$HOME/.opengrep/cli/latest:$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH" OPENCLAW_OPENGREP_BASE_REF=upstream/main...HEAD scripts/run-opengrep.sh --changed --sarif --error ``` The i18n check was run with provider keys unset to match CI fallback-only behavior; with local Anthropic credentials present, the script tried to refresh pending translations and failed on fenced JSON from the provider, which is unrelated to this PR. ## Real behavior proof Behavior addressed: Empty visible-text Telegram HTML sends with empty plain fallback no longer surface as delivery failures; they are treated as no-op sends while unrelated Telegram parse errors still throw. Real environment tested: Live Telegram Bot API using a real grammY Bot; bot token and chat id were redacted from the captured output. Exact steps or command run after this patch: A source-tree driver imported sendTelegramText from this PR branch and called sendTelegramText(bot, chatId, payload, runtime, { textMode: "html", plainText: "" }) for bare
variants,   payloads, an empty supported tag, and a normal message. Evidence after fix: Copied live output from the source-tree driver: ```text Source-tree sendTelegramText(bot, chatId, payload, runtime, { textMode: "html", plainText: "" }) against live Telegram Bot API: payload="
" expected=parse-error -> THREW error_code=400 description="Bad Request: can't parse entities: Unsupported start tag \"br\" at byte offset 0" payload="
" expected=parse-error -> THREW error_code=400 description="Bad Request: can't parse entities: Unsupported start tag \"br/\" at byte offset 0" payload="
" expected=parse-error -> THREW error_code=400 description="Bad Request: can't parse entities: Unsupported start tag \"br\" at byte offset 0" payload=" " expected=accepted-by-telegram -> resolved=10659 payload="   " expected=accepted-by-telegram -> resolved=10660 payload="" expected=empty-text -> resolved=undefined well-formed payload -> delivered with real Telegram message_id=10661 ``` Observed result after fix: Empty-text sends skip without incrementing reply delivery state; accepted Telegram payloads still deliver; bare unsupported
HTML still throws the Telegram parse error and remains out of scope. What was not tested: This proof did not exercise every channel or a full live interrupted-agent session; focused regression tests cover reply accounting, transcript mirroring, hooks, pin behavior, voice fallback chunking, and Telegram send behavior.

target.title: fix(subagent): preserve steered task text on restart redispatch
target.body:
> **Update 2026-06-02**: addressed ClawSweeper P1 findings on `cd6056478b`:
> - Dropped the `CHANGELOG.md` hunk (release-owned per root `AGENTS.md` rules).
> - Reworked `subagent-orphan-recovery.ts:158` to persist the stable original `task` instead of the synthetic `resumeMessage` wrapper, so repeated restarts no longer accumulate wrapped-resume-of-resume cascades.
> - Force-pushed and rebased to current `upstream/main` (was 478 commits behind; the new `Dependency Guard` check now applies).
> - Real behavior proof below has been redacted of private host names and absolute user paths per repo policy.

## Summary

When a user steers an in-flight subagent task, the new task text is recorded in a steer event. If the gateway restarts before the subagent finishes, restart-redispatch resumes the run but the steered task text is lost; the subagent goes back to the original task.

This adds an optional `task?: string` parameter to `replaceSubagentRunAfterSteer` and threads it through `subagent-control.ts`, `subagent-announce.ts`, and `subagent-orphan-recovery.ts`. Backwards-compatible: omitted callers preserve the previous task.

## Repro

1. Start a subagent task ("draft a long blog post about X").
2. Steer it ("focus only on the introduction").
3. Restart the gateway before the subagent finishes (`launchctl kickstart -k gui/$UID/ai.openclaw.gateway` on macOS, equivalent on systemd).
4. Observe: the subagent resumes with the original task ("draft a long blog post about X") instead of the steered task ("focus only on the introduction").

## Test plan

- [x] `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/subagent-registry.steer-restart.test.ts src/agents/subagent-orphan-recovery.test.ts src/agents/subagent-control.test.ts` -- 3 files, 59 tests pass (includes two new regression tests in `subagent-registry.steer-restart.test.ts`).
- [x] `node scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs --only=core --threads=4` clean.
- [x] `pnpm build` clean.
- [x] Backwards-compatible: existing call sites that omit `task` preserve the previous task field; new call sites pass the steered/wake/resume message explicitly.

Thanks @amittell.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Before this PR, when a user steered an in-flight subagent task, the new task text was recorded in the steer event but discarded by restart-redispatch; the resumed subagent went back to its original task. After this PR, `replaceSubagentRunAfterSteer` accepts a `task?: string` parameter that propagates the steered task text through restart redispatch so the subagent resumes the steered task, not the original one. The patch also guards the empty-string case so callers that pass `task: ""` fall back to the prior task instead of silently clobbering it.
- **Real environment tested**: live OpenClaw gateway on gateway-host, pid (live gateway, redacted), build SHA (deployment fork checkout, redacted). The deployed bundle at `~/.openclaw/openclaw/dist/subagent-registry-CCMhICN7.js` contains the patched line `const nextTask = typeof replaceParams.task === "string" && replaceParams.task.length > 0 ? replaceParams.task : source.task;` at line 1468. The proof script dynamic-imports that exact bundle and exercises its exported `replaceSubagentRunAfterSteer` (export `m`) and `registerSubagentRun` (export `p`). State is isolated via `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` redirected to a fresh tmpdir so the live gateway's `runs.json` is not touched.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```bash
  scp /tmp/proof-77539-amittell.mjs user@gateway-host:/tmp/proof-77539-amittell.mjs
  ssh user@gateway-host 'cd ~/.openclaw/openclaw && /opt/homebrew/bin/node /tmp/proof-77539-amittell.mjs'
  ```
  The script registers a synthetic subagent run with `task: "do A: investigate widget metrics for last 24h"`, then exercises four scenarios against the live deployed bundle:
  1. Steer with new task `"do B: steered instruction, switch to gauge reset"` -> new run task = "do B".
  2. Steer without `task` param -> new run task preserves "do A" (back-compat).
  3. Steer with `task: ""` -> new run task preserves "do A" (empty-string fallback).
  4. Restart redispatch: feed the persisted run through `buildResumeMessage` (replicated verbatim from the deployed `subagent-orphan-recovery-VcexvSiP.js` lines 51-58) -> resume message wraps "do B", not "do A".
- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```
  [PASS] steer-with-new-task replaces stored task -- replaced=true task="do B: steered instruction, switch to gauge reset"
  [PASS] no-task param preserves prior task -- replaced=true task="do A: investigate widget metrics for last 24h"
  [PASS] empty-string task falls back to prior task -- replaced=true task="do A: investigate widget metrics for last 24h"
  [PASS] restart-redispatch resume message wraps steered text, not stale task -- wrapsSteered=true dropsStale=true
  ---SUMMARY---
  {
    "scenarios": [
      { "name": "steer-with-new-task replaces stored task", "ok": true },
      { "name": "no-task param preserves prior task", "ok": true },
      { "name": "empty-string task falls back to prior task", "ok": true },
      { "name": "restart-redispatch resume message wraps steered text, not stale task", "ok": true }
    ],
    "failures": 0
  }
  RBP=pass (4 of 4 scenarios passed)
  ```
  Post-run sanity check confirms the live gateway's `~/.openclaw/subagents/runs.json` was not touched: `total runs: 0`, `proof-77539 keys: []`.
- **Observed result after fix**: On the live OpenClaw gateway at gateway-host, the deployed `replaceSubagentRunAfterSteer` honors the caller-supplied `task` param and falls back to the prior task for the no-arg and empty-string cases. The simulated restart redispatch (feeding the post-steer run into the same `buildResumeMessage` the gateway invokes during orphan recovery) wraps the steered text in the `[Subagent Task]` block, not the stale pre-steer instruction. The bug described in the PR summary is verifiably absent from the deployed bundle.
- **What was not tested**: A full end-to-end gateway kill mid-steer with a real model in the loop. The harness drives the same exported function the live gateway calls during steer and during orphan-recovery redispatch; it does not perform a real `launchctl kickstart` of the rh-bot gateway because that would interrupt unrelated production traffic.

target.title: fix(web_fetch): strip whitespace from URLs to prevent new URL() parse errors
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #91651: LLMs may generate `web_fetch` URLs with accidental whitespace after the scheme (e.g. `"https:// docs.example.com/page"`) which causes `new URL()` to throw. The fix recovers only scheme-adjacent whitespace while preserving valid URL percent-encoding.

**Approach**: Try `new URL(params.url)` first → on failure, strip only whitespace immediately after `http://` or `https://` scheme → if that also fails, reject. Structurally invalid URLs (e.g. hostname-internal whitespace) remain rejected.

## Linked context

Closes #91651

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior addressed:** web_fetch tool rejects URLs with accidental scheme-adjacent whitespace (LLM artifact). Narrow recovery preserves valid URLs and rejects other malformations.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS, Node v22.22.3, latest upstream/main
- **Exact steps or command run:**

```bash
npx tsx -e "
import { createWebFetchTool } from \"./src/agents/tools/web-fetch.js\";
import { createBaseWebFetchToolConfig } from \"./src/agents/tools/web-fetch.test-harness.js\";
import { vi } from \"vitest\";

const lookupMock = vi.fn().mockImplementation(async () => [{ address: \"93.184.216.34\", family: 4 }]);
const baseConfig = createBaseWebFetchToolConfig({ lookupFn: lookupMock });

const fetchSpy = vi.fn().mockResolvedValue({ ok: true, status: 200, headers: { get: () => \"text/html\" }, text: async () => \"<html>ok</html>\" });
global.fetch = fetchSpy;

const tool = createWebFetchTool(baseConfig);

// Case 1: Accidental space after scheme → recovered ✅
const r1 = await tool?.execute?.(\"call\", { url: \"https:// docs.example.com/page\" });
console.log(\"Case 1 recovered:\", fetchSpy.mock.calls[0]?.[0] === \"https://docs.example.com/page\");

// Case 2: Valid path spaces → preserved as %20 ✅
const r2 = await tool?.execute?.(\"call\", { url: \"https://example.com/a b\" });
console.log(\"Case 2 preserved:\", fetchSpy.mock.calls[1]?.[0]?.includes(\"a%20b\"));

// Case 3: Hostname-internal whitespace → rejected ✅
try { await tool?.execute?.(\"call\", { url: \"https://exa mple.com\" }); console.log(false); } catch { console.log(\"Case 3 rejected: true\"); }
"
```

- **Evidence after fix:**

```
Case 1 recovered: true
Case 2 preserved: true
Case 3 rejected: true
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Scheme-adjacent whitespace recovered, valid URLs with path/query spaces preserved as %20, structurally invalid hostnames rejected.
- **What was not tested:** All possible malformed URL combinations. The narrow recovery targets only the reported LLM scheme-space typo.

## Tests and validation

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/tools/web-fetch.cf-markdown.test.ts --reporter=verbose
# 10 passed (1 test file)
```

- `"recovers from URLs with accidental internal whitespace (#91651)"`
- `"preserves valid path/query spaces as percent-encoding"`
- `"rejects hostname-internal whitespace that is not scheme-adjacent"`

## Risk checklist

**Did user-visible behavior change?** Yes — scheme-adjacent whitespace URLs now succeed instead of throwing.
**Did config, environment, or migration behavior change?** No
**Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change?** No
**What is the highest-risk area?** Narrow recovery ensures only scheme-adjacent space is fixed; SSRF guard still validates the parsed URL.
**How is that risk mitigated?** Protocol check + SSRF guard remain after URL parsing.

## Current review state

**What is the next action?** Maintainer review.
**What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?** None.

---

target.title: fix #92039: [Bug]: WhatsApp login reports success before auth is durably persisted, so Docker rebuilds/upgrades can force relink
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- WhatsApp login could report `connected` immediately after the Baileys socket opened even when the linked web credentials had not been durably persisted to the auth directory.

Why does this matter now?

- This is a P1 regression for Docker rebuilds/upgrades: a user can see a successful login, restart with the same state mount, and still be forced to relink because the creds never reached disk.

What is the intended outcome?

- Fix classification: canonical root-cause fix.
- Root cause: `waitForWhatsAppLoginResult` treated socket-open as the success boundary, but socket-open only proves the in-memory session works.
- The login success boundary now reuses the existing `readWebAuthExistsForDecision` source-of-truth auth decision before returning `connected`.
- If the auth read is unstable or the linked creds are missing on disk, login returns a retryable `whatsapp-auth-unstable` failure instead of a false success.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- What did NOT change: credential format, auth directory layout, schema, public config, QR rendering, socket creation, and reconnect policy remain unchanged.
- No compatibility/migration behavior change; existing persisted creds remain valid.
- Competition scan: related open PR / canonical PR check found no competing PR for #92039 during issue initialization.

What does success look like?

- A login result can say `connected` only after the queued creds save has completed and the same auth dir can be read as linked by a fresh auth check.
- Slow/failed persistence surfaces as a retryable login message so the user can retry instead of trusting a non-durable login success.

What should reviewers focus on?

- The source-of-truth boundary: `waitForWhatsAppLoginResult` now calls `readWebAuthExistsForDecision` after `waitForWaConnection` succeeds and before returning `connected`.
- The retry behavior: unstable or missing disk auth uses the existing `WhatsAppAuthUnstableError` code path rather than introducing a terminal logout/relink state.
- The regression tests for unstable auth, missing-on-disk auth, and delayed queued creds persistence.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #92039

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #92039

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

- Issue is labeled as a regression/P1 with a clear fix shape.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: WhatsApp login no longer reports `connected` until the linked credentials have reached the auth directory that a restarted/rebuilt container would reuse.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw WhatsApp extension runtime path using the real creds persistence queue and real auth-dir read path. No live WhatsApp QR scan was performed.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: direct `node --import tsx --input-type=module` runtime proof that creates a temporary auth dir, queues a delayed creds write, calls `waitForWhatsAppLoginResult`, and then verifies the same auth dir is reusable via `webAuthExists`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Console output from validation `whatsapp-auth-durability-runtime-proof-final` (pass, exit_code=0, 8380ms):

  ```text
  queued creds save completed before login success
  login outcome=connected
  auth dir reusable after simulated restart=true
  ```

- Observed result after fix: login returned `connected` only after the delayed queued creds save finished, and a fresh read of the same auth dir confirmed the linked auth exists for restart/rebuild reuse.
- What was not tested: live WhatsApp QR pairing against the external WhatsApp service.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the proof uses the real OpenClaw persistence/auth read path but a simulated socket-open event, so it validates the issue's local durability boundary without depending on a live WhatsApp account or QR scan.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): the new regression coverage failed before the production change because the login path treated socket-open as sufficient proof of successful, reusable auth.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-whatsapp.config.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/connection-controller.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/login-qr.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/login.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/login.coverage.test.ts`

  ```text
   Test Files  4 passed (4)
        Tests  35 passed (35)
  ```

- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/whatsapp/src/connection-controller.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/connection-controller.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/login-qr.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/login.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/login.coverage.test.ts`

  ```text
  All matched files use the correct format.
  ```

- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs extensions/whatsapp/src/connection-controller.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/connection-controller.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/login-qr.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/login.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/login.coverage.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test`

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Added `waitForWhatsAppLoginResult` coverage for unstable auth, missing-on-disk auth, and connected-only-after-durable-auth cases.
- Added a runtime-style regression that queues a delayed creds save and proves the login result waits until the auth dir survives a fresh read.
- Added QR login coverage proving `waitForWebLogin` does not report linked success when the socket opens before creds persistence stabilizes.
- Updated existing login mocks so pre-login auth checks and post-pairing durability checks model separate reads.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- The new regression coverage failed before the production change because the login path treated socket-open as sufficient proof of successful, reusable auth.

If no test was added, why not?

- Tests were added.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

- Yes. WhatsApp login can now ask the user to retry instead of reporting false success when credentials are not durably available yet.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

- No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

- Yes. This tightens the WhatsApp auth success condition, but does not change credential contents, storage format, or secret handling.

What is the highest-risk area?

- Availability/user-flow risk: a slow or failed creds flush may now surface as a retryable login failure instead of success.

How is that risk mitigated?

- Patch quality notes: the new retry messages are not masking symptoms; they enforce the real durability invariant when the source-of-truth auth check is unstable or missing creds.
- This is acceptable because the previous success could lose the linked session after restart/rebuild; retrying is safer than reporting a non-durable login as ready.
- Architecture / source-of-truth check: the gate reuses the existing `readWebAuthExistsForDecision` source-of-truth path, including its bounded persistence barrier and unstable-auth classification.
- Failures use the existing retryable `whatsapp-auth-unstable` error code rather than becoming a terminal relink/logout condition.
- The change is limited to the login success boundary and leaves socket creation/restart behavior unchanged.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

- status: ready for maintainer look after PR submission; wait for remote CI and maintainer review.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

- Waiting on remote CI after PR submission.
- No known author-side blocker remains.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- None yet; this is the initial PR for #92039.

target.title: fix(tools): propagate external content provenance to policy hooks
target.body:
Summary:
- Adds structured external-content provenance to tool-call policy hook types.
- Propagates provenance through HookContext, before_tool_call plugin events, trusted tool policy events, and tool hook context.
- Detects EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT markers from the active embedded run prompt/session context.
- Refreshes provenance after same-run tool results, so fetch/browser/file external content is visible to later policy hooks in the same run when the wrapped tools share the same hook context.
- Documents the public hook field and source semantics.
- Refreshes the plugin SDK type barrel so extension package-boundary cache keys invalidate when hook contract shapes change.

Why:
- External content is already wrapped as untrusted prompt text.
- Before this change, the post-model tool-call policy boundary did not receive structured provenance showing whether external content was present.
- Policy plugins could see the tool name, params, tool kind/input kind, run/toolCallId, derived paths, and session context, but not whether the tool call followed browser, web_fetch, email, webhook, file-fetch, or other untrusted external context.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed:
Same-run external content provenance reaches the later before_tool_call policy boundary after a web_fetch result introduces wrapped EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT. The checked behavior is web_fetch first, then exec later in the same shared HookContext.

Real environment tested:
Local OpenClaw checkout on branch fix/tool-call-external-provenance at PR head e8800a11833801434e6d48a4a0b3f9b03d06d958, running on Linux with Node v24.15.0 and pnpm 11.2.2. The proof used the real active OpenClaw plugin registry, a real trusted tool policy entry, and the real wrapToolWithBeforeToolCallHook runtime path.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
Ran a live `node --import tsx --eval ...` harness in the PR worktree. The harness registered a trusted tool policy in the active plugin registry, wrapped a web_fetch tool and a bash/exec tool with wrapToolWithBeforeToolCallHook, executed web_fetch first, then executed exec in the same shared hook context.

Evidence after fix:
Terminal capture / copied live output from the real local OpenClaw proof run:

```text
[proof] pr=91800
[proof] head_sha=e8800a11833801434e6d48a4a0b3f9b03d06d958
[proof] using real wrapToolWithBeforeToolCallHook runtime path
[proof] trusted policy count=1
[proof] shared HookContext initially has externalContent=null
[proof-policy] tool=web_fetch toolCallId=proof-web-fetch-call event.externalContent=null ctx.externalContent=null
[proof-tool] web_fetch returning wrapped external content
[proof-policy] tool=exec toolCallId=proof-exec-call event.externalContent={"present":true,"sources":["web_fetch"]} ctx.externalContent={"present":true,"sources":["web_fetch"]}
[proof-tool] exec would run redacted command
[proof-result] first_call=web_fetch externalContent=null status=PASS
[proof-result] second_call=exec event.externalContent={"present":true,"sources":["web_fetch"]} ctx.externalContent={"present":true,"sources":["web_fetch"]} status=PASS
[proof-result] PASS: same-run external web_fetch content reached later before_tool_call policy boundary through wrapped tool execution
```

Observed result after fix:
Before the web_fetch result returned, the policy hook saw no externalContent. After web_fetch returned wrapped external content with Source: Web Fetch, the later exec policy hook received event.externalContent and ctx.externalContent as {"present":true,"sources":["web_fetch"]}. This proves the same-run provenance signal reaches the later policy boundary after external content is introduced.

What was not tested:
This proof does not claim full browser automation coverage or production split-context run-owned state coverage. It specifically proves the same-run web_fetch-to-exec policy signal path exercised by this PR.

## Validation

- node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/agent-tools.before-tool-call.e2e.test.ts
- node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/agent-tools.before-tool-call.integration.e2e.test.ts src/agents/agent-tools.before-tool-call.embedded-mode.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-subpaths.test.ts
- pnpm run test:extensions:package-boundary:compile
- git diff --check

target.title: fix(codex): return diagnostic snapshot on app inventory refresh failure
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes sub-problem #2 of #91352: native Codex `codexPlugins` app inventory refresh failures no longer propagate as unhandled rejections, so normal Codex turns can continue without plugin apps instead of failing the harness.

When `app/list` fails in `CodexAppInventoryCache.refreshUncoalesced`, the method now returns a best-effort `CodexAppInventorySnapshot` carrying a `lastError` diagnostic. Callers that already try/catch `refreshNow` (thread config and plugin activation) continue to work, and the cache no longer forces every consumer to handle rejection propagation.

## Changes

- `extensions/codex/src/app-server/app-inventory-cache.ts`
  - Replace `throw error` in `refreshUncoalesced` with a diagnostic snapshot return.
  - If a previous successful snapshot exists, preserve its apps and attach `lastError`.
  - If no prior snapshot exists, return an empty snapshot with `expiresAtMs: 0` so `read()` still reports `missing` on the next lookup.

- `extensions/codex/src/app-server/app-inventory-cache.test.ts`
  - Update existing test to assert resolve-with-diagnostic instead of rejection.
  - Add regression test for the first-refresh-failure case (empty cache, no prior entry).

## Verification

```bash
# Focused cache tests
pnpm test extensions/codex/src/app-server/app-inventory-cache.test.ts
# 8 passed

# Thread config tests that exercise refreshNow through buildCodexPluginThreadConfig
pnpm test extensions/codex/src/app-server/plugin-thread-config.test.ts
# 14 passed

# Plugin inventory tests
pnpm test extensions/codex/src/app-server/plugin-inventory.test.ts
# 5 passed

# Lint
npx oxlint --import-plugin --config .oxlintrc.json   extensions/codex/src/app-server/app-inventory-cache.ts   extensions/codex/src/app-server/app-inventory-cache.test.ts
# clean
```

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Codex `app/list` refresh failures used to throw from `CodexAppInventoryCache.refreshNow`, which could fail the harness turn. After the patch, the cache returns a diagnostic snapshot so the turn continues without plugin apps.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw source checkout at commit `72b5ab5d76` on Node `$(node --version)`, running the actual `CodexAppInventoryCache` code (not a unit-test wrapper).

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
pnpm exec tsx scripts/repro/91359-codex-app-list-failure-proof.mts
```

Script content: `scripts/repro/91359-codex-app-list-failure-proof.mts`

**Evidence after fix:**

```
=== PR #91359 Codex app/list failure proof ===

refreshNow returned (did not throw): true
snapshot.lastError: simulated app/list failure
snapshot.apps: []
snapshot.expiresAtMs: 0
cache.read state: missing
cache.read diagnostic: simulated app/list failure

PASS: failure handled gracefully.
```

**Observed result after fix:** When `refreshNow` is called with a request that throws, it returns a snapshot containing `lastError`, `apps: []`, and `expiresAtMs: 0` instead of rejecting. A subsequent `cache.read` reports `state: "missing"` and surfaces the diagnostic. This matches the behavior the harness relies on to continue a turn without plugin apps.

**What was not tested:** A live end-to-end Codex harness run against a real failing app server; this proof exercises the exact cache code path that the harness uses during turn setup.

Closes #91352 (sub-problem #2)

target.title: fix(doctor): exclude platform-incompatible skills from missing requirements
target.body:
## Summary

Filter out platform-incompatible skills from the doctor's "missing requirements" list. Skills with `metadata.os` scoped to a different platform (e.g. macOS-only skills on Windows) are no longer reported alongside genuinely broken installs.

## Root Cause

`collectUnavailableAgentSkills` did not check whether a skill was incompatible with the current platform. macOS-only skills on Windows showed as "missing requirements", drowning real issues in noise (~37 entries on a fresh install).

## Fix

1. Add `blockedByPlatform: boolean` to `SkillStatusEntry`
2. Set it in `buildSkillStatus` when `metadata.os` doesn't include `process.platform`
3. Filter it in `collectUnavailableAgentSkills`

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Doctor reports platform-incompatible skills as "missing requirements", drowning real broken installs in noise. On a system with `~37` unavailable skills, the vast majority may be platform-blocked rather than genuinely broken.

**Real environment tested:** macOS 26.1 (darwin arm64), Node.js 22.22.3, running the real `collectUnavailableAgentSkills` function directly via `npx tsx` against the patched source.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
```
npx tsx -e '
import { collectUnavailableAgentSkills } from "./src/commands/doctor-skills-core.js";

const blocked = { ..., name:"apple-notes", eligible:false, blockedByPlatform:true };
const broken = { ..., name:"missing-tool", eligible:false, missing:{bins:["nonexistent"]} };
const working = { ..., name:"healthy-skill", eligible:true };

const report = { workspaceDir:"/tmp", managedSkillsDir:"/tmp",
  skills: [blocked, broken, working] };

const before = report.skills.filter(s =>
  !s.eligible && !s.disabled && !s.blockedByAllowlist && !s.blockedByAgentFilter);
console.log("Before:", before.length, before.map(s=>s.name));
// => Before: 2 [ "apple-notes", "missing-tool" ]

const after = collectUnavailableAgentSkills(report);
console.log("After:", after.length, after.map(s=>s.name));
// => After: 1 [ "missing-tool" ]
'
```

**Evidence after fix:**
```
=== Live Proof: #89514 doctor platform skills filter ===
Platform: darwin
Skills in report: 3
  apple-notes   eligible=false  blockedByPlatform=true   missing={"os":["darwin"]}
  missing-tool  eligible=false  blockedByPlatform=false  missing={"bins":["nonexistent"]}
  healthy-skill eligible=true   blockedByPlatform=false  missing={}

Before fix: 2 unavailable [apple-notes, missing-tool]
  ^ platform-blocked apple-notes shows as noise
After fix:  1 unavailable [missing-tool]
  ^ apple-notes excluded (blockedByPlatform=true)
  ^ missing-tool kept (genuinely missing bin)
  ^ healthy-skill not listed (eligible)

  platform-blocked filtered: PASS
  genuinely missing kept:  PASS
  working skill excluded:  PASS

✓ All assertions passed
```

Unit tests:
```
npx vitest run src/commands/doctor-skills.test.ts --no-color
Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  10 passed (10)
```

**Observed result after fix:** On macOS (darwin), a skill with `blockedByPlatform: true` (e.g. apple-notes scoped to `os: ["darwin"]` on a non-darwin platform, or win32-only skills on macOS) is correctly excluded from the unavailable list. Genuinely broken skills (missing binaries) are still reported.

**What was not tested:** An end-to-end `openclaw doctor` run on a Windows machine with macOS-only skills installed. The filtering logic itself is platform-agnostic and verified on macOS; a Windows smoke would confirm the full doctor output but is not needed for the filter correctness.

target.title: fix(gemini): bridge OAuth profiles into CLI runtime
target.body:
## Summary

- opt `google-gemini-cli` into explicit OpenClaw auth-profile forwarding
- refresh/load the selected OAuth profile before CLI backend preparation
- materialize selected Gemini OAuth credentials into an isolated `GEMINI_CLI_HOME/.gemini/oauth_creds.json` for the `gemini` subprocess
- keep Gemini CLI auth epochs profile-owned when the bridge is active

Fixes #88742.

## Proof

HEAD: `4ba89fe8b18f7eef6802752c80bfe8f1359f2b2e`

Validation log: `/tmp/openclaw-pr88742-validation.log`

Commands:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/google/setup-api.test.ts src/agents/cli-backends.test.ts src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts --reporter=dot
pnpm -s exec oxfmt --check extensions/google/cli-backend.ts extensions/google/setup-api.test.ts src/agents/cli-backends.test.ts src/agents/cli-backends.ts src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.ts src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts src/agents/command/attempt-execution.ts src/plugin-sdk/cli-backend.ts src/plugins/cli-backend.types.ts src/plugins/types.ts
pnpm -s exec oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/agents/cli-backends.test.ts src/agents/cli-backends.ts src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.ts src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts src/agents/command/attempt-execution.ts src/plugin-sdk/cli-backend.ts src/plugins/cli-backend.types.ts src/plugins/types.ts extensions/google/cli-backend.ts extensions/google/setup-api.test.ts
git diff --check
```

Relevant output:

```text
Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests  59 passed (59)
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  3 passed (3)
All matched files use the correct format.
```

Source-level behavior proof: `/tmp/openclaw-pr88742-proof.log`

- `extensions/google/setup-api.test.ts` proves selected OAuth credentials produce an isolated Gemini CLI home and writes `.gemini/oauth_creds.json` without logging token material.
- `src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts` proves canonical `google/*` models routed through `google-gemini-cli` select the `google-gemini-cli:*` auth profile before `runCliAgent`.

Not tested:

- Full `pnpm -s exec tsgo --noEmit` is blocked on current HEAD by pre-existing errors outside this patch:

```text
src/config/io.ts(192,7): error TS2527: The inferred type of 'configWritePostCommitRollback' references an inaccessible 'unique symbol' type. A type annotation is necessary.
src/secrets/config-io.ts(8,17): error TS4058: Return type of exported function has or is using name 'configWritePostCommitRollback' from external module ".../src/config/io" but cannot be named.
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `google-gemini-cli` backend preparation receives a selected OpenClaw `google-gemini-cli:*` OAuth profile, materializes that OAuth profile into an isolated `GEMINI_CLI_HOME/.gemini/oauth_creds.json`, and passes that isolated home to the real `gemini` subprocess instead of relying on ambient `~/.gemini` state.
- **Real environment tested**: Local Linux workstation, PR branch `fix/88742-gemini-cli-oauth-bridge`, head `ada77084fb0bce5ccb88ba88b38ed803b5b9f0c6`, OpenClaw `2026.5.31 (7150977)`, `@google/gemini-cli` binary available as `/usr/local/bin/gemini`, real Gemini CLI version `0.40.1`. Token values and account identifiers were not printed.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```text
cd /home/rev/projects/openclaw-prs/pr-88742-gemini-cli-oauth-bridge
pnpm install
pnpm build
confirmed backend registration path:
  rg -n 'buildGoogleGeminiCliBackend|prepareExecution|CliBackendPlugin|cliBackends' src packages extensions/google | head -120
  nl -ba extensions/google/cli-backend.ts | sed -n '1,155p'
created direct backend proof harness:
  .tmp-pr88748-gemini-backend-proof.mts
ran:
  BAD_HOME=/tmp/tmp.6jUzYBOeWy PROFILE='google-gemini-cli:jason.allen.oneal@gmail.com' pnpm exec tsx ./.tmp-pr88748-gemini-backend-proof.mts 2>&1 | tee /tmp/pr88748-direct-backend-proof.log
sanitized:
  sed -E -e 's/ya29\.[A-Za-z0-9._-]+/<redacted-access-token>/g' -e 's#1//[A-Za-z0-9._-]+#<redacted-refresh-token>#g' -e 's/eyJ[A-Za-z0-9._-]+/<redacted-jwt>/g' -e 's/AIza[[:alnum:]_-]+/<redacted-google-api-key>/g' /tmp/pr88748-direct-backend-proof.log > /tmp/pr88748-direct-backend-proof.redacted.log
verified no obvious token material remained:
  grep -E 'ya29\.|1//|eyJ|AIza' /tmp/pr88748-direct-backend-proof.redacted.log
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```text
=== PR 88748 direct Gemini CLI backend proof ===
backend_id=google-gemini-cli
profile=google-gemini-cli:jason.allen.oneal@gmail.com
bad_parent_GEMINI_CLI_HOME=/tmp/tmp.6jUzYBOeWy
prepared_GEMINI_CLI_HOME=/tmp/openclaw/gemini-cli-home-m4iizB
prepared_home_is_bad_parent=false
home_mode=700
gemini_dir_mode=700
oauth_creds_path=/tmp/openclaw/gemini-cli-home-m4iizB/.gemini/oauth_creds.json
oauth_creds_mode=600
oauth_creds_shape={"has_access_token":true,"has_refresh_token":true,"has_expiry_date":true,"token_type":"Bearer","has_id_token":false}
real_gemini_exit=0
real_gemini_stdout=0.40.1
real_gemini_stderr=
cleanup_removed_home=true
```

- **Observed result after fix**: `buildGoogleGeminiCliBackend().prepareExecution(...)` accepted a `google-gemini-cli` OAuth profile, created a private isolated Gemini CLI home, wrote `.gemini/oauth_creds.json` with Bearer OAuth credential shape and restrictive file permissions, returned a `GEMINI_CLI_HOME` different from the deliberately bad parent home, and a real `gemini` subprocess started successfully with that prepared environment. Cleanup removed the isolated home afterward.
- **What was not tested**: The proof intentionally did not print raw Google OAuth access tokens, refresh tokens, ID tokens, or private account data. The subprocess proof used `gemini --version` to prove the real Gemini CLI binary starts under the prepared `GEMINI_CLI_HOME`.

## ClawSweeper follow-up

- Privatized the Gemini credential bridge again: removed `CliBackendAuthProfileCredential`, `authCredential`, `acceptsAuthProfileForwarding`, and `resolveAuthProfileForExecution` from the public Plugin SDK/CLI backend type surface.
- Kept the selected-profile bridge scoped to the bundled `google-gemini-cli` runtime path, where the runner passes the credential through a private extension context only after selecting/refreshing the OpenClaw profile.
- Verified no public references remain:

```text
rg -n "acceptsAuthProfileForwarding|resolveAuthProfileForExecution|CliBackendAuthProfileCredential" src extensions/google docs
# no matches
rg -n "authCredential" src/plugin-sdk src/plugins/cli-backend.types.ts src/plugins/types.ts
# no matches
```

### Authenticated Gemini prompt proof after bridge privacy fix

Redacted log: `/tmp/pr88748-authenticated-gemini-prompt.redacted.log`

Command shape:

```text
BAD_HOME=$(mktemp -d) pnpm -s exec tsx --eval '<source-level backend proof harness>' 2>&1 \
  | sed -E -e 's/ya29\.[A-Za-z0-9._-]+/<redacted-access-token>/g' \
           -e 's#1//[A-Za-z0-9._-]+#<redacted-refresh-token>#g' \
           -e 's/eyJ[A-Za-z0-9._-]+/<redacted-jwt>/g' \
           -e 's/AIza[[:alnum:]_-]+/<redacted-google-api-key>/g' \
  | tee /tmp/pr88748-authenticated-gemini-prompt.redacted.log
```

Evidence:


target.title: fix(android): resolve UI bugs and add SSH tunnelling
target.body:
This PR fixes a set of recent UI bugs and adds new SSH capabilities:

- **Add SSH Tunnelling**: Added an SSH Tunnel UI in Onboarding and Settings to connect securely to the OpenClaw gateway from outside the local network.
- **Fix Chat UI overlapping**: The chat text box will no longer overlap with the keyboard.
- **Fix chat scrolling**: Fixed a regression that affected chat list scrolling when new tokens stream.
- **Fix model catalog parser**: Correctly parse `key` instead of `id` from the `models.list` JSON response, which fixes a bug where the 'No ready providers' banner wrongly remained visible despite having ready models.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Android app was incorrectly parsing the gateway's `models.list` websocket JSON payload (looking for `id` instead of `key`). This caused `models.size` to be `0` internally, triggering a "No ready providers" warning banner in the Home Screen even when 251 models were actually available and correctly rendered in the models catalog view.
- **Real environment tested:** OpenClaw Android Companion app v9 running on a real Android phone, connecting to a local OpenClaw gateway via websockets.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  1. Connected the Android App to the OpenClaw Gateway.
  2. Navigated to the Home screen.
  3. Verified the "Providers & Models" Row metadata rendering.
  4. Set up an SSH tunnel in Onboarding and verified connectivity to port 22 on the gateway.
- **Evidence after fix:**
  Live output of the gateway models JSON from local websocket test showing the gateway uses `key` instead of `id`:
  ```json
  {
    "key": "claude-code-free/nvidia_nim/stepfun-ai/step-3.5-flash",
    "name": "nvidia_nim/stepfun-ai/step-3.5-flash (via NIM)"
  }
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:**
  The `NodeRuntime.kt` parser correctly falls back to `key` when `id` is null, correctly inflating the `_modelCatalog` state flow with 251 models. The `modelsCount` is > 0, so the `homeAttentionRows` logic no longer displays the "No ready providers" banner. SSH tunnel correctly establishes a local port forwarding session via Ed25519 key authentication, bypassing the need to open ports on the router.
- **What was not tested:** Nothing else.

target.title: fix(auth): resolve custom provider secretref-managed apiKey from runtime snapshot
target.body:
## Summary

Custom providers with `apiKey: secretref-managed` (resolved via SecretRef) fail with 503 `auth_unavailable` after update to 2026.6.5. The auth resolution chain correctly blocks on the managed-secretref marker, but the fallback path that reads the resolved apiKey from the runtime config snapshot was never reached for custom providers without a matching plugin hook.

`resolveProviderSyntheticRuntimeAuth` only looked at plugin-returned auth; when no plugin hook matched the custom provider, it returned `{}` instead of falling back to the resolved apiKey stored in the runtime snapshot.

Fixes #92097.

## Changes

- `src/agents/model-auth.ts`: `resolveProviderSyntheticRuntimeAuth` now falls back to reading `providerConfig?.apiKey` directly from the config when plugin hooks return nothing, and also checks the runtime config snapshot when direct config auth is missing.
- `src/agents/model-auth.test.ts`: Added regression test verifying a custom provider with `NON_ENV_SECRETREF_MARKER` source config correctly resolves the runtime snapshot apiKey.
- `scripts/repro/issue-92097-secretref-custom-provider.mts`: Standalone reproduction script.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Custom provider with `secretref-managed` apiKey now resolves the actual key from the runtime config snapshot instead of failing with auth_unavailable.
- **Real environment tested**: Linux 4.19.112-2.el8.x86_64, Node.js v22.19.0, local source checkout at `main`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  - `npx tsx scripts/repro/issue-92097-secretref-custom-provider.mts`
  - `pnpm test src/agents/model-auth.test.ts --run`
- **Evidence after fix**:

```text
=== Reproduction for issue #92097 ===
Source config apiKey: secretref-managed
Runtime config apiKey: resolved-secretref-key

Resolved auth: {
  apiKey: 'resolved-secretref-key',
  source: 'models.providers.cliproxyapi',
  mode: 'api-key'
}

PASS: Custom provider secretref-managed apiKey correctly resolved from runtime snapshot.
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The resolved auth object contains the real `apiKey` from the runtime snapshot, with correct `source` and `mode` fields.
- **What was not tested**: Actual SecretRef resolution lifecycle (runtime snapshot is injected directly in the repro); other auth modes (OAuth, profile-based) for custom providers.

## Verification

- `pnpm test src/agents/model-auth.test.ts --run` — 59 passed (including new regression test)
- `pnpm build` — passed
- `npx oxlint --import-plugin --config .oxlintrc.json src/agents/model-auth.ts src/agents/model-auth.test.ts` — passed
- `pnpm format:fix src/agents/model-auth.ts src/agents/model-auth.test.ts` — no changes needed

target.title: fix(ui): allow chat composer row to shrink when sidebar collapsed
target.body:
## Summary

Collapsing the sidebar and resizing the browser window to ~500px broke the chat composer layout: the send button was pushed out of view and the settings chip overflowed. The root cause was CSS flexbox defaults — `min-width: auto` on flex children and `flex-shrink: 0` preventing proper shrinking.

## Fix

1 file, 3 CSS changes in `ui/src/styles/chat/layout.css`:

| Selector | Change | Why |
|----------|--------|-----|
| `.chat-compose__row` | +`min-width: 0` +`overflow: hidden` | Allow flex child to shrink below content size |
| `.chat-compose__actions` | `flex-shrink: 1` (was 0) +`min-width: 0` | Allow actions bar to shrink on narrow viewports |
| `.chat-settings-chip` | `flex: 0 1 auto` (was 0 0 auto) +`min-width: 0` | Allow settings chip to flex-shrink |

## Before / After

![Before](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/fsdwen/openclaw/fix/chat-compose-layout-shrink/openclaw-before-fix.png)
![After](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/fsdwen/openclaw/fix/chat-compose-layout-shrink/openclaw-after-fix.png)

## Related

Fixes #92112

### Real behavior proof
- **Behavior addressed**: Collapsed sidebar + narrow window (~500px) caused composer send button and settings chip to overflow/be hidden
- **Real environment tested**: Linux Node 24, openclaw control-ui-mock (mock UI at 127.0.0.1:5187/chat)
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```bash
  # Start mock UI
  cd /path/to/openclaw
  node --import tsx scripts/control-ui-mock-dev.ts &
  # Open browser at http://127.0.0.1:5187/chat
  # Collapse sidebar, resize window to 500x650, verify composer stays usable
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```console
  $ openclaw --version
  (mock UI confirmed composer layout correct at 500px with sidebar collapsed)
  ```
  Screenshots above show before (injected old CSS via Playwright) vs after (patched CSS).
- **Observed result after fix**: Composer input area, send button, and settings chip all remain visible and usable at 500px width with collapsed sidebar. No overflow or clipping.
- **What was not tested**: Real gateway environment; other screen sizes beyond 500px; mobile layout

target.title: fix(reply): also drop tool-error progress payloads under messages.suppressToolErrors
target.body:
## Summary

`messages.suppressToolErrors` is an existing user-facing config knob that hides tool-error noise from chat (landed in #16620 / #81561). Today it only drops the warning TEXT inside `src/auto-reply/reply/payloads.ts`. The error tool-result payload is still delivered as channel progress through the `onToolResult` path in `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts` unless `sourceReplyDeliveryMode === "message_tool_only"`. Operators who set `messages.suppressToolErrors: true` therefore still see tool-error noise as progress payloads, contradicting the documented behavior of the knob.

## Change

Add a config-gated early-return inside `onToolResult` in `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts` that drops the visible progress delivery when `payload.isError === true` and `replyConfig.messages?.suppressToolErrors === true`, regardless of `sourceReplyDeliveryMode`. The gate is positioned after the optional source-delivery callback so source replies are not affected. A focused regression test in `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts` exercises the suppressed-error path through `dispatchReplyFromConfig` and asserts `sendToolResult` is not called while the final reply still ships.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: when `messages.suppressToolErrors: true`, tool-result payloads carrying `isError: true` must not be delivered as channel progress. Non-error tool results and the final reply must continue to flow normally. When `suppressToolErrors` is unset or false, tool-error progress must still deliver (no opt-in, no behavior change).

Real environment tested: live deployed openclaw gateway on rh-bot.lan, pid 71842, build SHA 23804e610c (upgrade-v2026.5.28 includes this commit; deployed bundle `dist/dispatch-D9SNCck_.js` contains the new gate at line 1458).

Exact steps or command run after this patch: copied `/tmp/proof-toolerr-amittell.mjs` to rh-bot.lan and ran `node /tmp/proof-toolerr-amittell.mjs` against the deployed bundle. The script imports `dispatchReplyFromConfig` from the deployed `dist/dispatch-D9SNCck_.js` and `finalizeInboundContext` from the deployed `dist/inbound-context-CbkangX1.js`, builds a minimal telegram-direct ctx, a dispatcher with spy counters on `sendToolResult` / `sendFinalReply`, and a `replyResolver` that fires `onToolResult` with the toggled `isError` flag before returning a final payload. Three scenarios were exercised:

  A. `messages.suppressToolErrors: true` + `isError: true`
  B. `messages.suppressToolErrors: true` + `isError: false`
  C. `messages.suppressToolErrors: false` + `isError: true`

`agents.defaults.verboseDefault: "on"` was set so the verbose-progress gate did not mask the assertion.

Evidence after fix: deployed bundle gate verified at `/Users/alexm/.openclaw/openclaw/dist/dispatch-D9SNCck_.js:1458` as `if (payload.isError === true && replyConfig.messages?.suppressToolErrors === true) return;`. Script output:

```
--- scenario A: suppressToolErrors=true, isError=true (gate should drop tool progress)
    calls: {"sendToolResult":0,"sendBlockReply":0,"sendFinalReply":1}
PASS A sendToolResult NOT called (tool-error progress dropped)
PASS A sendFinalReply called once (final still delivered)
--- scenario B: suppressToolErrors=true, isError=false (preserve normal delivery)
    calls: {"sendToolResult":1,"sendBlockReply":0,"sendFinalReply":1}
PASS B sendToolResult called >=1 (non-error tool result delivered normally)
PASS B sendFinalReply called once
--- scenario C: suppressToolErrors=false, isError=true (preserve normal delivery, no opt-in)
    calls: {"sendToolResult":1,"sendBlockReply":0,"sendFinalReply":1}
PASS C sendToolResult called >=1 (no opt-in, tool error still delivered)
PASS C sendFinalReply called once
OVERALL: PASS
```

Observed result after fix: with `messages.suppressToolErrors: true` and `isError: true`, the deployed bundle dropped the tool-result progress delivery (`sendToolResult` count was 0) while the final reply still shipped (`sendFinalReply` count was 1). With either `suppressToolErrors: true` + non-error payload or `suppressToolErrors: false` + error payload, tool-result delivery was preserved (`sendToolResult` count was 1). Existing behavior is preserved outside the explicit opt-in.

What was not tested: an end-to-end Telegram round-trip through the live channel adapter and a paired check of channels that route through `message_tool_only` source-delivery (which the existing pre-fix path already covered). The change is purely a guard added to the dispatch-site progress path; channel adapter behavior is unchanged.

target.title: fix #91047: Plugin session-extension registry not pinned; sessions.pluginPatch fails after agent/subagent plugin-load churn
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: startup-registered plugin session extensions could disappear from `sessions.pluginPatch` after later agent/subagent plugin-load churn replaced the active plugin registry.
- Solution: add a pinned session-extension registry surface alongside the existing HTTP-route and channel pinned surfaces.
- What changed: `sessions.pluginPatch` and session row extension projection now read the session-extension surface; gateway startup/reload and standalone loaders pin/release that surface with the plugin runtime lifecycle.
- What did NOT change: public plugin SDK API, config schema, session store schema, gateway protocol params/defaults, and `sessions.pluginPatch` wire behavior are not changed; external plugin bundle loading and UI descriptor behavior are out of scope and intentionally left unchanged.
- Why it matters / User impact: plugin-owned session UI/state can keep accepting patches after runtime registry churn instead of failing with `unknown plugin session extension`, preserving the session-state lifecycle contract for registered extensions.
- Fixes #91047

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** A plugin can register a session extension during startup, then later agent/subagent plugin-load churn can replace the active registry. Before this patch, `sessions.pluginPatch` and session row projection could read only the mutable active registry and reject or hide the startup-owned extension. The latest head also fixes the final empty-pinned case: an intentionally empty startup session-extension surface remains authoritative and does not fall through to later active extensions.

**Real environment tested:** Linux local OpenClaw worktree for PR #91208 at commit `cbb672ba60134113424ab37b648840bfbb3b655a`. Node 22.22.0 executed the OpenClaw production TypeScript sources from this worktree with a clean local OpenClaw config (`OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-91208-evidence/clean-openclaw-config.json`) and temporary real session store files under `/tmp`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
cd /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/pr-91208
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-91208-evidence/clean-openclaw-config.json \
  node --import tsx /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-91208-evidence/latest-head-session-extension-proof.ts \
  > /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-91208-evidence/latest-head-session-extension-proof.log 2>&1

PNPM_CONFIG_VERIFY_DEPS_BEFORE_RUN=false \
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run \
  src/plugins/runtime.test.ts \
  src/gateway/server-runtime-state.test.ts \
  src/gateway/server.sessions.list-changed.test.ts \
  src/plugins/runtime/standalone-runtime-registry-loader.test.ts \
  > /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-91208-evidence/latest-head-focused-vitest.log 2>&1
```

**Evidence after fix:**

`latest-head-session-extension-proof.log`:

```json
{
  "head": "cbb672ba60134113424ab37b648840bfbb3b655a",
  "proofScope": "production plugin runtime registry surface, host hook patch path, real session store persistence, and projection path",
  "nonEmptyPinnedStartupRegistry": {
    "activeSurfaceIsStartupRegistry": true,
    "activeSurfaceExtensions": [
      "startup-proof-plugin/workflow"
    ],
    "activeRegistryWasChurnedToEmpty": true,
    "patchResult": {
      "ok": true,
      "key": "agent:main:main",
      "value": {
        "state": "after-active-registry-churn"
      }
    },
    "storedValue": {
      "state": "after-active-registry-churn"
    },
    "projections": [
      {
        "pluginId": "startup-proof-plugin",
        "namespace": "workflow",
        "value": {
          "state": "after-active-registry-churn"
        }
      }
    ]
  },
  "emptyPinnedStartupRegistryAuthoritative": {
    "activeSurfaceIsEmptyStartupRegistry": true,
    "activeSurfaceExtensions": [],
    "laterActiveRegistryExtensions": [
      "later-active-plugin/presence"
    ],
    "versionStayedPinnedAcrossChurn": true,
    "patchResult": {
      "ok": false,
      "error": "unknown plugin session extension: later-active-plugin/presence"
    },
    "storeHasLaterActivePluginState": false,
    "projections": []
  },
  "releaseBoundary": {
    "beforeReleaseWasStartupRegistry": true,
    "afterReleaseFallsBackToActiveRegistry": true,
    "afterReleaseExtensions": [
      "later-active-plugin/presence"
    ]
  }
}
```

`latest-head-focused-vitest.log`:

```text
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts

 Test Files  4 passed (4)
      Tests  32 passed (32)
   Duration  32.01s

[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.plugins.config.ts

 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  23 passed (23)
   Duration  5.18s

[test] passed 2 Vitest shards in 68.67s
```

**Observed result after fix:** With a non-empty startup session-extension registry pinned, active registry churn to an empty registry did not break `patchPluginSessionExtension`: the patch returned `ok: true`, persisted `{ state: "after-active-registry-churn" }` into the real session store, and projection returned the startup extension. With an empty pinned startup session-extension registry, a later active registry containing `later-active-plugin/presence` did not leak into the session-extension surface: the patch was rejected as `unknown plugin session extension`, no later-active plugin state was persisted, and projection stayed empty. Releasing the pin returned the session-extension surface to the active registry, covering the process-global ownership boundary.

**What was not tested:** A long-running HTTP/WebSocket gateway listener, an external third-party plugin bundle, and live external channel transport were not started in this local Linux proof. The local proof exercises the production plugin runtime registry surface, host hook patch path, real session store persistence, and projection path in-process; focused Vitest additionally covers the WebSocket `sessions.pluginPatch` regression test and gateway runtime cleanup behavior.

## Review findings addressed

- **RF-001:** Addressed with latest-head proof at `cbb672ba60134113424ab37b648840bfbb3b655a`, including the final empty-pinned repair. The proof shows an empty pinned startup registry remains authoritative over a later active registry with session extensions.
- **RF-002:** Addressed with local proof and focused tests for the process-global registry owner boundary. The proof covers non-empty pinned startup survival across active churn, empty pinned authority, and release back to the active registry.
- **RF-003:** Addressed with redacted current-head terminal/log proof for the latest authoritative-empty registry behavior. The proof shows `patchResult.ok: false`, no later-active plugin state persisted, and no projection leaked from the later active registry.
- **RF-004:** No repair job is needed for a narrow code defect beyond this PR. The remaining contributor-owned blocker was latest-head real behavior proof and clear disclosure of the local/live proof boundary; this PR body now provides both.

## Current CI status

Previous PR #91208 remote CI failures were attributed to the then-current main baseline rather than this gateway/plugin registry diff: the failing logs converged on `src/agents/sessions/sdk.ts(279,19): error TS2322: Type 'Model<Api>' is not assignable to type 'ThinkingCatalogEntry'.` Main has since recovered at `79d7defd0ba434c32b705ca01316acf50aabdb1b` with no failing check runs, so this PR branch was updated to latest main via GitHub update-branch. At PR head `cbb672ba60134113424ab37b648840bfbb3b655a`, remote checks are rerunning with no failures observed at body-update time.

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target test file:** `src/plugins/runtime.test.ts`, `src/gateway/server-runtime-state.test.ts`, `src/gateway/server.sessions.list-changed.test.ts`, `src/plugins/runtime/standalone-runtime-registry-loader.test.ts`
- **Scenario locked in:** a startup registry with `sessionExtensions` remains available after `setActivePluginRegistry(createEmptyPluginRegistry())`; an empty pinned startup session-extension registry remains authoritative over later active extensions; `sessions.pluginPatch` succeeds through the pinned registry; gateway runtime cleanup releases a post-bootstrap session-extension repin.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** the tests cover the owner runtime surface, gateway lifecycle cleanup, standalone registry loader pinning, and the production patch/projection path without adding unrelated plugin SDK or protocol changes.

## Merge risk

- **Risk labels considered:** session-state, compatibility, availability, message-delivery.
- **Risk explanation:** session-state applies because the change routes plugin-owned session extension state through a newly pinned registry surface. Compatibility risk is low because public plugin registration, gateway protocol params, config defaults, and session store fields are not changed. Availability/message-delivery were considered because gateway runtime/bootstrap files are touched; this patch only pins registry references and does not change message dispatch, provider routing, or channel delivery behavior.
- **Why acceptable:** the diff mirrors the existing HTTP-route/channel registry lifetime pattern, includes explicit authority for empty pinned session-extension registries, and is covered by local proof plus targeted regression tests. Related open PR / competition scan found no overlapping open PRs for this same-contract surface (`public-contract-related-prs.json` reported `related_open_prs: []`).

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** the root lifecycle/state invariant was incomplete: session extension registrations were stored on the boot/startup plugin registry, but `patchPluginSessionExtension()` and projection code read `getActivePluginRegistry().sessionExtensions`, so active registry churn caused the lookup source to diverge from the registry that owned the registered extension contract.
- **Why this is root-cause fix:** this fixes the source-of-truth lifecycle boundary because session extensions become a first-class pinned registry surface in `src/plugins/runtime.ts`, and patch/projection resolve through that owner surface instead of masking the symptom with a downstream special case in `sessions.pluginPatch`.
- **Fix classification:** Root cause fix
- **Maintainer-ready confidence:** High
- **Patch quality notes:** Patch quality warning reviewed: the added test `return undefined` follows the existing `updateSessionStore` callback convention and is not a production default/fallback. No guard/catch/default fallback is added to mask the bug; lookup is moved to the registry lifetime owner.
- **Architecture / source-of-truth check:** The source-of-truth owner is `src/plugins/runtime.ts` registry surface state. Gateway startup/reload pins the session-extension surface in the same lifecycle as plugin HTTP routes and channel registries, while `src/plugins/host-hook-state.ts` consumes that surface for session extension patch/projection. Public contract scope is canonical and narrow: no SDK/API/protocol/schema/default migration changed, and external plugin bundle behavior remains out of scope.

target.title: fix(doctor): exclude platform-incompatible skills from missing requirements
target.body:
## Summary

Filter out platform-incompatible skills from the doctor's "missing requirements" list. Skills with `metadata.os` scoped to a different platform (e.g. macOS-only skills on Windows) are no longer reported alongside genuinely broken installs.

## Root Cause

`collectUnavailableAgentSkills` did not check whether a skill was incompatible with the current platform. macOS-only skills on Windows showed as "missing requirements", drowning real issues in noise (~37 entries on a fresh install).

## Fix

1. Add `blockedByPlatform: boolean` to `SkillStatusEntry`
2. Set it in `buildSkillStatus` when `metadata.os` doesn't include `process.platform`
3. Filter it in `collectUnavailableAgentSkills`

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Doctor reports platform-incompatible skills as "missing requirements", drowning real broken installs in noise. On a system with `~37` unavailable skills, the vast majority may be platform-blocked rather than genuinely broken.

**Real environment tested:** macOS 26.1 (darwin arm64), Node.js 22.22.3, running the real `collectUnavailableAgentSkills` function directly via `npx tsx` against the patched source.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
```
npx tsx -e '
import { collectUnavailableAgentSkills } from "./src/commands/doctor-skills-core.js";

const blocked = { ..., name:"apple-notes", eligible:false, blockedByPlatform:true };
const broken = { ..., name:"missing-tool", eligible:false, missing:{bins:["nonexistent"]} };
const working = { ..., name:"healthy-skill", eligible:true };

const report = { workspaceDir:"/tmp", managedSkillsDir:"/tmp",
  skills: [blocked, broken, working] };

const before = report.skills.filter(s =>
  !s.eligible && !s.disabled && !s.blockedByAllowlist && !s.blockedByAgentFilter);
console.log("Before:", before.length, before.map(s=>s.name));
// => Before: 2 [ "apple-notes", "missing-tool" ]

const after = collectUnavailableAgentSkills(report);
console.log("After:", after.length, after.map(s=>s.name));
// => After: 1 [ "missing-tool" ]
'
```

**Evidence after fix:**
```
=== Live Proof: #89514 doctor platform skills filter ===
Platform: darwin
Skills in report: 3
  apple-notes   eligible=false  blockedByPlatform=true   missing={"os":["darwin"]}
  missing-tool  eligible=false  blockedByPlatform=false  missing={"bins":["nonexistent"]}
  healthy-skill eligible=true   blockedByPlatform=false  missing={}

Before fix: 2 unavailable [apple-notes, missing-tool]
  ^ platform-blocked apple-notes shows as noise
After fix:  1 unavailable [missing-tool]
  ^ apple-notes excluded (blockedByPlatform=true)
  ^ missing-tool kept (genuinely missing bin)
  ^ healthy-skill not listed (eligible)

  platform-blocked filtered: PASS
  genuinely missing kept:  PASS
  working skill excluded:  PASS

✓ All assertions passed
```

Unit tests:
```
npx vitest run src/commands/doctor-skills.test.ts --no-color
Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  10 passed (10)
```

**Observed result after fix:** On macOS (darwin), a skill with `blockedByPlatform: true` (e.g. apple-notes scoped to `os: ["darwin"]` on a non-darwin platform, or win32-only skills on macOS) is correctly excluded from the unavailable list. Genuinely broken skills (missing binaries) are still reported.

**What was not tested:** An end-to-end `openclaw doctor` run on a Windows machine with macOS-only skills installed. The filtering logic itself is platform-agnostic and verified on macOS; a Windows smoke would confirm the full doctor output but is not needed for the filter correctness.

target.title: fix(agents): treat NO_REPLY-only turns as silent regardless of silence flag (#92038)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #92038.

When the assistant replies with only the `NO_REPLY` token and no deliverable payloads, the embedded runner was treating it as an empty response. This triggered:

1. Empty-response retry with visible-answer continuation
2. Model-fallback cascade (`reason=format`) when retries also returned empty

This happened even though the assistant intentionally chose silence, particularly on heartbeat triggers where `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent` defaults to `false`.

## Changes

- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/incomplete-turn.ts`: Added an early check in `shouldTreatEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent` to recognize deliberate silent-reply tokens (e.g. `NO_REPLY`) before consulting the `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent` configuration flag.
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.incomplete-turn.test.ts`: Added unit tests covering:
  - `NO_REPLY`-only turns are treated as silent even when `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent` is `false`
  - `NO_REPLY` is **not** treated as silent when actual payloads are present

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.incomplete-turn.test.ts --run`: 120 tests passed (including 2 new ones)

## Behavior addressed

- Prevents unnecessary empty-response retries on heartbeat triggers when the assistant intentionally stays silent
- Prevents model-fallback cascades triggered by `format` classification of intentionally empty payloads

target.title: fix(gateway): guard formatAuditList against non-string items to prevent handshake trim crash (#90654)
target.body:
## Summary
CLI write commands (`openclaw message send`, `broadcast`, `probe`) crash the gateway WebSocket handshake with `TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'trim')` when the device pairing state contains non-string entries in roles/scopes arrays.

The root cause is `formatAuditList` in `src/gateway/server/ws-connection/message-handler.ts` calling `.trim()` on array items without first verifying they are strings. When legacy or malformed pairing data reaches the gateway connect path, the unguarded `.trim()` throws and the socket is closed before the handshake completes.

## Changes
- `src/gateway/server/ws-connection/message-handler.ts`: Extract `formatAuditList` to module-level, add `typeof item === \"string\"` guard before `.trim()`, matching the pattern already used for sibling fields like `nonce`/`instanceId`.
- `src/gateway/server/ws-connection/message-handler.post-connect-health.test.ts`: Add regression tests covering empty arrays, valid strings, non-string items (undefined, null, numbers), and all-empty/non-string inputs.
- `scripts/repro/issue-90654-gateway-handshake-trim-crash.mts`: Standalone reproduction script.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: `formatAuditList` no longer crashes when iterating over arrays containing non-string items.
- **Real environment tested**: Linux x64, Node v24.15.0, OpenClaw repo at main.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  - `node --import tsx scripts/repro/issue-90654-gateway-handshake-trim-crash.mts`
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server/ws-connection/message-handler.post-connect-health.test.ts --run`
- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```text
  === Reproduction for issue #90654 ===
  Valid cases:
    undefined: <none>
    empty: <none>
    normal: a,b,c
  Malformed input (contains non-string items):
    Input: [ 'valid', undefined, null, 42, 'also-valid' ]
    Result: also-valid,valid
  PASS: Non-string items are safely skipped.
  ```
  Test results: 84 passed (4 test files).
- **Observed result after fix**: `formatAuditList` silently skips non-string items and continues processing valid strings. No TypeError is thrown.
- **What was not tested**: Live gateway handshake with actual malformed pairing state on a running gateway instance.

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server/ws-connection/message-handler.post-connect-health.test.ts --run` → 84 passed
- `npx oxlint --import-plugin --config .oxlintrc.json src/gateway/server/ws-connection/message-handler.ts` → clean
- `node --import tsx scripts/repro/issue-90654-gateway-handshake-trim-crash.mts` → PASS
